easy.companyDescription: A mature enterprise software company shifting more of its business toward cloud subscriptions.
easy.macroContext: Businesses are moving more computing work from owned infrastructure to rented cloud services.
easy.situation: Can a large installed base revive growth through subscriptions before faster rivals define the market?
easy.longCase: Long-standing corporate relationships could accelerate adoption of cloud and subscription offerings.
easy.shortCase: Mature product lines may shrink before replacements contribute enough growth to satisfy investors.
setupHints: Years of investor frustration raise the bar for whether this pivot looks credible.

medium.companyDescription: A technology company with a broad commercial customer base.
medium.macroContext: Business customers are reviewing established suppliers as budgets shift between operating needs.
medium.situation: Can new spending from existing customers offset slower demand for older offerings?
medium.longCase: A broad customer base could support additional sales without proportionate selling costs.
medium.shortCase: Budget pressure and competitive alternatives could limit the payoff from a changing mix.

hard.companyDescription: A technology company serving commercial customers.
hard.macroContext: Companies are scrutinizing technology budgets and comparing the cost of established suppliers.
hard.situation: Can a changing sales mix support growth while customers reassess spending?
hard.longCase: Repeat customers could make incremental sales more efficient than winning entirely new accounts.
hard.shortCase: Slower renewals and competitive pricing could weigh on revenue before the new mix proves itself.

reveal.shortText: That was Microsoft. The cloud pivot under Satya Nadella started changing how investors valued the old software giant.
reveal.funFact: By early 2016, the market had started rewarding the company for cloud growth and recurring software revenue.
whyItMoved: Cloud and subscription revenue became more visible.
Investors began re-rating recurring software streams.
Enterprise relationships helped distribution of newer offerings.

sources.label: Prototype sample seed
sources.notes: Placeholder sample content for Phase 3 pipeline; not production research.

review.reviewNotes: H029 rewrote Medium and Hard after H028 linked a diversified enterprise vendor, 2014 refresh, customer relationships, and changing purchase models to Microsoft. Medium peer set: Microsoft/Oracle/IBM/SAP. Hard peer set: IBM/Oracle/SAP/Cisco/Hewlett-Packard/Microsoft; the prose concerns generic commercial budget and renewal pressure. Easy and shared labels remain unchanged; Medium/Hard Gate 2 results were removed as stale.

decisionUseful: competitive pricing during a sales-mix change

Enterprise technology vendor competing as customers reprice established suppliers
